# Tap.Giving -- Full Knowledge Base

> The most affordable NFC tap-to-give plates for churches. One-time purchase, no monthly fees, no transaction fees, no app required. Works with any giving platform.

Website: https://tap.giving
Email: hello@tap.giving

Location: Spring, TX (Houston area)

## 1. What Is Tap.Giving?

Tap.Giving sells custom-printed NFC (Near Field Communication) plates that churches mount on pews, chairs, or walls. When a congregant taps their smartphone on the plate, it instantly opens the church's existing giving page in their phone's browser. No app download, no account creation, no QR code scanning -- just tap and give.

Tap.Giving is hardware, not software. We are not a payment processor. Churches keep whatever giving platform they already use (Tithely, Pushpay, Givelify, Donorbox, Subsplash, Planning Center, Anedot, Realm, or any platform with a web giving URL). Our plates simply provide a new physical entry point to their existing giving system.

### Core Value Proposition

- One-time hardware cost -- no monthly subscription fees
- No transaction fees from Tap.Giving (processing fees come from the church's chosen giving platform)
- Platform agnostic -- works with any giving platform that has a web-based giving page
- No app required for donors -- visitors and members can give without downloading anything
- Frictionless -- tap-to-give takes 2-3 seconds, compared to 15-30 seconds for text-to-give or 30-60 seconds for typing a URL
- First-time visitor friendly -- 53% of NFC givers are first-timers who would never download an app
- Built by a pastor -- founded by Ryan Shook, an active pastor who built Tap.Giving for his own church

---

## 2. Product Details and Specifications

Product page: https://tap.giving/tap-to-give-plate.html

- Plates are 100mm (4 inches) in diameter
- Material: PVC
- NFC chip: NTAG215 (industry standard, compatible with all modern smartphones)
- Full-color custom printing with church logo and branding
- Adhesive backing included on all plates (3M adhesive)
- Additional mounting options: pre-drilled screw holes, elastic bands for chairs
- Built-in QR code backup on every plate for 100% device compatibility
- NFC chips are passive -- no batteries, no charging, no maintenance, no Wi-Fi needed
- URL can be locked (tamper-proof, recommended) or left unlocked (reprogrammable)
- Available in multiple colors to match church branding
- Designed for long-term mounting -- waterproof and scratch-resistant

### What's Included in Every Order

- Custom-printed NFC plates with church logo
- 3M adhesive backing on all plates
- Built-in QR code backup
- Setup guide with step-by-step installation instructions
- One year of email support
- Pre-drilled screw holes available upon request
- Elastic bands available for chair mounting

---

## 3. Pricing

### Volume Pricing Tiers

| Quantity | Price Per Plate | Example Total |
|----------|----------------|---------------|
| 100-199 plates | $4.50 | $450 (100 plates) |
| 200-399 plates | $4.00 | $800 (200 plates) |
| 400+ plates | $3.50 | $1,400 (400 plates) |

### What's Included

- Minimum order: 100 plates
- Free shipping on all orders (continental US)
- No setup fees
- No monthly fees -- ever
- No transaction fees from Tap.Giving
- Custom branding included at every tier
- Design proof sent for approval before production
- Promo code WELCOME10 gives 10% off first order
- 30-day satisfaction guarantee

### Delivery Timeline

- Orders typically arrive within 3-5 weeks
- This includes production time for custom-branded plates plus shipping
- Rush orders available upon request
- Ships via UPS with tracking provided

### Savings Compared to Competitors

| Competitor | Annual Cost | Tap.Giving (One-Time) |
|-----------|------------|----------------------|
| Pushpay | $17,700/year + transaction fees | $450 (100 plates) |
| Overflow Tap | $2,500-$10,000/year + hardware | $800 (200 plates) |
| Tithely Plus | $1,428/year + 2.9% fees | $1,400 (400 plates) |
| Tap.Giving | $0/year after purchase | One-time purchase |

### Reordering

Additional plates can be ordered anytime. Tap.Giving keeps the church's branding design on file for easy reordering.

---

## 4. How It Works

### For the Church (Setup)

1. Church fills out the order form with their logo, giving URL, and quantity
2. Tap.Giving sends a design proof for approval
3. Tap.Giving prints custom-branded plates, encodes them with the church's giving URL, and ships free
4. Church mounts plates on pews or chairs with adhesive backing, screws, or elastic bands
5. Setup takes 5-10 minutes -- everything is pre-configured before shipping

### For the Giver (3-Step Process)

**Step 1: Tap Your Phone**
Hold your smartphone near any Tap.Giving plate. The phone automatically detects the NFC signal and opens the giving page instantly. Works with iPhone 6+ and Android 4.4+ without any app downloads.

**Step 2: Choose Amount**
Select a giving amount from preset options or enter a custom amount. Add a note if desired. Everything is secure and encrypted.

**Step 3: Complete Donation**
Complete the secure donation with Apple Pay, Google Pay, or any major credit/debit card (Visa, MasterCard, American Express, Discover). Average donation time: 15-30 seconds. Recurring giving options available through the church's giving platform.

### Why It Works

- No app download required -- works natively on modern smartphones
- No account creation -- visitors can give immediately
- No typing -- the URL loads automatically
- Works in dim sanctuaries -- unlike QR codes, no camera or line-of-sight needed
- Feels natural -- people already tap their phones to pay at stores

---

## 5. Founder Story

Tap.Giving was founded by Ryan Shook, a pastor at a large church in the Houston, Texas area. Here is his story in his own words:

"I'm a pastor. And like most pastors, I've watched my church's budget get squeezed from every direction -- utilities, insurance, maintenance, staff. So when it came time to explore digital giving, the last thing I wanted was another monthly subscription.

But that's exactly what I found. Platform after platform charging $50-$200 a month -- just to collect donations that were already being processed through Tithely, Subsplash, or Planning Center. I watched other churches agonize over the same decision: 'Do we really add one more subscription to an already tight budget?'

Then it hit me. NFC technology is remarkably simple. A chip stores a URL. A phone reads it. That's it. There's no reason this needs a $100/month platform. No reason it needs a login, a dashboard, or a sales call. It just needs to work.

So I built Tap.Giving. The concept is straightforward: buy the plates once, point them at your existing giving page, and never pay another fee. Your congregation taps their phone during the offering, their giving page opens, and they give -- right there in the moment, together.

The mission isn't to replace your giving platform. It's to give your congregation a physical, communal way to access it. Because the offering moment matters -- and it shouldn't cost your church $100 a month to keep it alive."

### Values Behind Tap.Giving

**Churches Shouldn't Overpay for Simplicity.** NFC is a $0.50 chip. The technology that makes tap-to-give possible is inexpensive and widely available. There's no reason it should cost a church $100 a month. The price should reflect the simplicity.

**The Offering Moment Matters.** Digital giving is great, but it shouldn't kill the communal act of passing the plate. There's something powerful about a congregation giving together in the same moment. Tap.Giving keeps both -- the convenience of digital and the togetherness of the offering.

**No Lock-In, Ever.** The plates work with any giving platform -- Tithely, Subsplash, Planning Center, Pushpay, Givelify, or any other. Switch providers whenever you want. The plates stay the same.

**Every Church Deserves This.** From a 30-person church plant to a 5,000-seat megachurch, the price per plate is the same. No enterprise tiers. No "call us for a quote." No sales calls required. Simple, fair pricing for every church.

---

## 6. NFC Technical Specifications

### What Is NFC?

NFC stands for Near Field Communication. It's a short-range wireless technology that allows two devices to exchange data when they're very close together -- within about 4 cm (1.5 inches). It operates at 13.56 MHz, a frequency standardized globally for contactless communication since the early 2000s. It is the same technology behind Apple Pay and Google Pay.

### How NFC Giving Works

For church giving, two NFC modes matter: passive tags (the plate) and active readers (the phone). A Tap.Giving plate contains an NTAG chip -- a tiny integrated circuit connected to a small antenna coil. The chip has no battery and no power source of its own. When a phone's NFC reader gets close enough, it generates an electromagnetic field that powers the chip via electromagnetic induction. The chip wakes up, reads its stored data (a URL), and transmits it back to the phone. The phone's operating system recognizes the URL and prompts the user to open it.

The entire exchange happens in milliseconds. No pairing. No Wi-Fi. No Bluetooth. No app. Just proximity and physics.

### NFC vs. Other Wireless Technologies

| Feature | NFC | Bluetooth | QR Code | Wi-Fi |
|---------|-----|-----------|---------|-------|
| Range | ~4 cm | ~10 m | Camera distance | ~50 m |
| Speed to connect | <0.1 seconds | 2-5 seconds | 1-3 seconds | 3-10 seconds |
| Requires app? | No | Yes | Camera app | No (browser) |
| Power source | None (passive) | Battery | None (printed) | AC power |
| Reliability | Very high | Moderate | Depends on lighting | Depends on signal |

### NFC Chip Specifications

- Chip type: NTAG215
- Memory: 504 bytes (sufficient for URL storage)
- Read cycles: 100,000+ (at 100 taps per Sunday, this is 19+ years)
- Lifespan: Virtually unlimited -- no moving parts, no battery to degrade
- Data format: NDEF (NFC Data Exchange Format)
- Protocol: ISO 14443-3A

### Do NFC Plates Need Batteries?

No. NFC giving plates use passive NFC chips. The chip has no battery and no power source. It is powered entirely by the electromagnetic field generated by the phone's NFC reader at the moment of the tap. There is nothing to charge, nothing to replace, and nothing that depletes over time.

### Do NFC Plates Need Wi-Fi or Cellular?

The plate itself does not need any network connection. The NFC exchange between the chip and the phone is a direct, local radio interaction. However, the phone does need data connectivity (Wi-Fi or cellular) to load the giving page after the tap. This is the same requirement as any online giving method.

---

## 7. Phone Compatibility

### iPhone

NFC tag reading has been built into every iPhone since the iPhone 7 (2016), running iOS 11 or later.

- **iPhone XS and later (2018+):** Background tag reading -- the phone reads NFC tags natively without any app or action. Just hold the phone near the plate and a notification appears automatically. This is the seamless experience.
- **iPhone 7, 8, and X:** NFC reading works, but the user needs to open the NFC Tag Reader from Control Center first. One extra step, but these phones are increasingly rare in 2026.

### Android

NFC has been available on most Android phones since Android 4.4 KitKat (2013). On Android, NFC tag reading is native and automatic -- no extra app, no special mode. Just tap and go. The main exception: some budget Android phones (typically under $100) may not include NFC hardware.

### Coverage

- 90%+ of smartphones sold since 2020 include NFC hardware
- Over 95% of smartphones currently in use support NFC
- 0 apps to download on modern iPhones and all NFC-equipped Androids

### What If Someone's Phone Doesn't Have NFC?

Nothing bad happens. The plate simply doesn't respond -- there's no error message, no awkward beep, no confused screen. Every Tap.Giving plate includes a QR code backup that works with any smartphone camera, ensuring 100% compatibility across all devices.

### Will Phone Cases Block NFC?

Standard cases -- plastic, leather, silicone, TPU -- do not block NFC signals. NFC operates through these materials without any issue. The only cases that can interfere are very thick metal cases or wallets with built-in RFID-blocking material, which represent less than 1% of cases in use.

### Will Tapping Trigger Apple Pay or Google Pay?

No. NFC tags and NFC payment terminals use different protocols. Apple Pay and Google Pay only activate when the phone detects a payment terminal that initiates a specific EMV contactless payment handshake. An NFC tag sends an NDEF record -- a completely different data format. Tapping a giving plate will never accidentally trigger any payment wallet.

---

## 8. NFC Security

### The Chip Contains Only a URL

No financial data. No personal information. No donor records. The NFC chip stores a single URL -- nothing more. It's functionally identical to a hyperlink printed on a piece of paper, except it's delivered wirelessly.

### Payment Processing Happens Elsewhere

All payment processing occurs on the church's giving platform's secure servers -- Tithely, Subsplash, Planning Center, Pushpay, or whichever service the church uses. These platforms are PCI-DSS compliant and handle encryption, tokenization, and fraud detection. The NFC plate is just the doorway.

### Locked Chips Prevent Tampering

Tap.Giving plates use locked NTAG chips by default. Once programmed, the data cannot be overwritten, erased, or modified by anyone -- not with an app, not with specialized hardware. The chip is permanently set.

### HTTPS Encryption

The redirect URL uses HTTPS, meaning the connection between the user's phone and the giving page is encrypted in transit. This is the same encryption standard used by banks and e-commerce sites.

### 4 cm Range Makes Interception Impossible

NFC operates at a maximum range of about 4 cm. To intercept an NFC communication, an attacker would need to be physically within inches of the transaction -- effectively impossible in a room full of people.

### NFC vs. QR Codes on Security

- **NFC plates (locked):** Tamper-proof. Cannot be overwritten. Secure HTTPS redirect.
- **QR codes:** Can be covered with a malicious sticker redirecting to a phishing page. No built-in tamper detection.

---

## 9. Locked vs. Unlocked NFC Chips

### Option 1: Locked (Recommended -- Tap.Giving Default)

Tap.Giving encodes each plate with a URL, then makes the NFC memory read-only. Anyone can still tap and open the link, but the data cannot be overwritten. Benefits:

- Prevents anyone with an NFC phone from overwriting the church's message
- Keeps donor journeys consistent months after deployment
- Makes physical maintenance easy -- no spot-checking for tampering

### Lock-Then-Redirect Method (Best Practice)

1. Provide a branded URL you control (e.g., give.yourchurch.org/sunday)
2. Tap.Giving programs that URL and locks the chip so it cannot be rewritten
3. Use your website or domain registrar to redirect the URL to any current campaign page
4. Change the redirect anytime without touching the physical plate

This gives permanent chip security with on-demand destination flexibility.

### Option 2: Unlocked (Requires Password)

Leaving plates unlocked means any person with physical access and an NFC-enabled phone can rewrite the destination. If choosing this option:

1. Download the free NFC Tools app for iOS or Android
2. Tap Other then Set Password, create a short passcode, and touch the plate to save it
3. When editing is needed, choose Remove Password, enter the code, make changes, then set the password again

Important: Passwords only block rewriting -- they do not stop scanning or guarantee full tamper resistance.

---

## 10. The Full Technical Flow (What Happens When Someone Taps)

From the moment a phone gets close to the plate to the moment the giving page loads, about two seconds pass:

1. **Phone detects electromagnetic field** -- The phone's NFC antenna senses the passive chip's presence when it enters the ~4 cm range.
2. **Phone powers the NTAG chip** -- The phone's NFC reader generates a radio field that powers the passive chip via electromagnetic induction. No battery needed.
3. **Chip transmits its NDEF record** -- The chip sends back its stored data -- an NDEF record containing a URL -- to the phone.
4. **Phone's OS reads the NDEF record** -- iOS or Android recognizes the record as a URL type and prepares to handle it.
5. **Phone displays a notification** -- A banner or prompt appears asking the user if they'd like to open the URL. On Android, this may open automatically.
6. **User taps the notification** -- One tap on the notification -- the only user action in the entire flow.
7. **Browser opens the giving page** -- The phone's default browser opens the church's giving page.
8. **User completes donation** -- The giver enters their amount and payment method through the giving platform's secure interface. Done.

What's NOT in this flow: No app download. No account creation. No Bluetooth pairing. No QR code scanning. No typing a URL. No Wi-Fi required at the point of tap. The entire interaction is one physical gesture + one screen tap.

---

## 11. Mounting and Installation

### Mounting Options

- **Adhesive back (standard):** 3M adhesive included on all plates. Peel and stick. Most popular option.
- **Screws:** Pre-drilled holes available upon request. Tap.Giving can ship screws.
- **Elastic bands:** For chairs without solid backs. Black only, ~111.8 cm for standard chairs.
- **Velcro:** Available but requires sewing to band.
- **Pins:** Option for fabric chairs.
- **Nano double-sided tape:** Extra adhesive option for additional strength.

### Recommended Placement Locations

- Chair/pew backs (most popular)
- Entry/exit areas
- Connection tables
- Lobby displays
- Bulletins or programs (with QR code)
- Children's ministry check-in

### Important Note

NFC does not work through metal surfaces. Plates must be mounted on non-metal materials (wood, plastic, fabric, etc.).

### How Many Plates Per Pew?

Most churches use 1-3 plates per pew or seating row for optimal coverage. Recommended: start with every other row or strategic high-traffic areas, then expand based on usage.

---

## 12. Compatible Giving Platforms

Tap.Giving works with any giving platform that has a web-based giving page. Confirmed compatible platforms include:

- Tithely (free tier and paid tiers)
- Pushpay
- Givelify
- Donorbox
- Subsplash
- Planning Center Giving
- Anedot
- Realm (ACS Technologies)
- Breeze ChMS
- Nucleus
- Any platform with an online giving URL

Setup guides are available on the Tap.Giving blog for each platform.

---

## 13. Competitive Comparison -- NFC Tap-to-Give Providers

### Tap.Giving vs. Competitors (NFC Hardware)

| Provider | Type | Price | Monthly Cost | Transaction Fee | Platform Lock-in |
|----------|------|-------|-------------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Tap.Giving** | NFC plates | $3.50-$4.50/plate (one-time) | $0 | $0 from us | None -- works with any platform |
| ChurchTap | NFC hardware | Not publicly listed | Unknown | Unknown | Unknown |
| Overflow Tap | NFC discs + subscription | Requires Overflow subscription | $208-$833/mo | Varies | Yes -- Overflow platform |
| Subsplash Tap | Proprietary NFC | Included with Subsplash subscription | Varies | Varies | Yes -- Subsplash platform |
| Clearstream | NFC tags | $1-$2/tag | $0 | None from tags | None |
| SecureGive Tap | NFC with platform | Requires SecureGive subscription | Varies | Varies | Yes -- SecureGive platform |

### Tap.Giving vs. Giving Platforms (Software Subscriptions)

| Solution | Type | Monthly Cost | Annual Cost | Transaction Fee | NFC Option |
|----------|------|-------------|------------|----------------|-----------|
| **Tap.Giving** | Hardware only | $0 | $0 after purchase | $0 from us | Yes (core product) |
| Pushpay | SaaS platform | ~$1,475/mo | ~$17,700/year | 2.9% + $0.30 | VisitorTap (limited) |
| Tithely | SaaS + free tier | $0-$119/mo | $0-$1,428/year | 2.9% + $0.30 | No native NFC |
| Overflow | SaaS platform | $208-$833/mo | $2,500-$10,000/year | Varies | Overflow Tap discs |
| Subsplash | SaaS platform | Varies | Varies | Varies | Subsplash Tap |
| Givelify | Mobile app | $0 | $0 | 2.9% + $0.30 | No |
| Donorbox | SaaS + free tier | $0-$150/mo | $0-$1,800/year | 2.95% platform fee | TapTag option |
| Anedot | Payment processor | $0 | $0 | 3.3% + $0.30 | No |
| Planning Center | ChMS with giving | Varies by module | Varies | Varies | No native NFC |

### Key Differentiator

Tap.Giving is hardware, not software. It does not compete with giving platforms -- it works alongside them. Churches keep their existing platform and add Tap.Giving plates as a physical entry point. This means no vendor lock-in and no additional monthly fees.

---

## 14. Church Giving Technology Overview

### The Landscape in 2026

- 49% of church giving is now done via credit card or other digital means
- 80% of all donations are made by debit or credit card
- 57% of churches reporting increased giving attributed growth to digital channels
- Churches offering multiple digital giving options are nearly 2x as likely to report increased giving
- Cash and check giving has declined to about 40% of all church donations
- 51% of smartphone users download zero apps per month
- Only 24% of regularly attending churchgoers made even one digital donation in the previous year

### Every Giving Method Compared

| Method | Setup Cost | Monthly Cost | Donor Friction | Visitor Friendly | Best For |
|--------|-----------|-------------|---------------|-----------------|---------|
| Cash / Checks | $0 | $0 | Medium | Only if they carry cash | Older congregations |
| Online Giving Page | $0-$500 | $0-$150 + fees | Medium | Only if they know the URL | Remote giving, recurring gifts |
| Mobile App | $0-$500 | $0-$1,475 + fees | High | No -- visitors won't download an app | Committed members |
| Text-to-Give | $0-$200 | $19-$200 + fees | Medium | Somewhat -- if announced | Mid-size budgets |
| QR Codes | $0-$50 | $0 | Medium | Somewhat -- if visible | Print materials, low budget |
| NFC Tap-to-Give | $350-$1,400+ | $0 | Very Low | Yes -- no download, no account | In-service giving, visitors |
| Giving Kiosk | $1,500-$3,000+ | $149+ | High | Somewhat -- lobby only | Large church lobbies |
| Digital Wallets | $0 | $0 | Very Low | Yes (if wallet set up) | Payment step optimization |

### Category Winners

- **Best for In-Service Giving:** NFC Tap-to-Give (Tap.Giving) -- lowest friction, works during worship
- **Best for Digital Infrastructure:** Online Giving Platforms (Tithely for free tier, Pushpay for enterprise)
- **Best for Simplicity:** Text-to-Give -- everyone knows how to text
- **Best for Free/DIY:** QR Codes -- zero cost, print anywhere
- **Best for Lobbies:** Giving Kiosks -- visible presence, card acceptance
- **Best for Integration:** All-in-One ChMS (Planning Center for modular, Subsplash for app-centric)

### NFC + Digital Wallets = The Fastest Path to a Gift

The combination of NFC tap-to-give and digital wallets creates the lowest-friction giving experience possible. A congregant taps the NFC plate, lands on the giving page, and completes the gift with Apple Pay or Google Pay -- all in under 15 seconds, without typing a single character.

---

## 15. Building a Church Giving Strategy by Size

### Small Church (Under 200 Attendance)

Total first-year cost: ~$450-$500

1. **NFC plates** on every seat ($450 for 100 plates from Tap.Giving)
2. **Tithely free tier** for online/recurring giving ($0/month)
3. **QR code** on bulletin as backup (free)
4. **Cash option** for those who prefer it

### Mid-Size Church (200-500 Attendance)

Total first-year cost: ~$800-$3,200

1. **NFC plates** on every seat ($800 for 200 plates)
2. **Tithely Plus** or **Donorbox** for online giving
3. **Text-to-give** (often included with platform)
4. **QR code** on bulletin as backup

### Large Church (500+ Attendance)

Total first-year cost: ~$1,400-$20,000+

1. **NFC plates** on every seat ($1,400 for 400 plates)
2. **Pushpay** or **Planning Center** for enterprise giving
3. **Text-to-give + kiosk** in the lobby
4. **QR codes** on signage and event materials

### The Common Thread

NFC plates appear in every recommended stack because no other technology captures the moment of generosity during worship as effectively. The other tools handle what happens outside the service. NFC handles the 60 seconds during the offering when people are most moved to give.

---

## 16. Statistics and Data Points

### NFC Giving Impact

- 300%+ increase in donations at point of collection with NFC
- 81% participation rate with NFC giving
- 53% of NFC givers had never given to that church before
- 3x higher average donation compared to cash
- NFC is 42x more effective than QR codes for engagement
- Average NFC donation time: 15-30 seconds
- Tap-to-give takes 2-3 seconds from tap to giving page

### Church Giving Trends

- 49% of church giving is now digital
- 80% of all donations made by debit or credit card
- 57% of growing churches cite digital giving as the driver
- Churches with multiple giving options are 2x as likely to see growth
- 60% of churchgoers say they are willing to give digitally
- 41% of total church donations are digital where offered
- Cash giving has declined to about 40% of all church donations
- Only 31% of churches saw growth in cash/check giving (2015-2025)
- 4% of church donations currently come through digital wallets (growing fast)

### App Adoption Reality

- 51% of smartphone users download zero apps per month
- Only 24% of regularly attending churchgoers made a digital donation in the previous year
- About 20% of digital church donations come through apps
- Nearly half of downloaded apps are uninstalled within 30 days

### Market Context

- Church giving tech market: ~$500M-$1.5B by 2033
- 12% CAGR (compound annual growth rate)
- 74% of churches now offer digital giving

---

## 17. Complete FAQ

### General Product Questions

**What is NFC giving?**
NFC (Near Field Communication) giving allows churchgoers to donate by tapping their smartphone on an NFC-enabled plate. The tap opens the church's giving page directly in the phone's browser -- no app download or account creation required.

**Does Tap.Giving process payments?**
No. Tap.Giving sells the NFC hardware. Payment processing happens through whatever giving platform the church already uses (Tithely, Pushpay, Givelify, etc.). We are hardware, not software.

**What phones work with NFC giving?**
Every iPhone since iPhone 7 (2016) and most Android phones since 2015 have NFC built in. This covers over 95% of smartphones in use today. No app download required on modern devices.

**What if someone doesn't have NFC on their phone?**
Every plate includes a QR code that works with any smartphone camera. This ensures 100% compatibility across all devices, regardless of NFC capability.

**How do people know to tap their phone on the plate?**
Each plate comes with clear instructions and a QR code backup. Most people are familiar with NFC from contactless payments (Apple Pay, Google Pay). Tap.Giving also provides instructional materials for the congregation.

**Will tapping trigger Apple Pay or Google Pay?**
No. NFC tags and payment terminals use different protocols. Apple Pay and Google Pay only activate with payment terminals. An NFC tag sends a URL -- a completely different data format. Tapping a giving plate will never accidentally trigger any payment wallet.

### Security Questions

**Is NFC giving secure?**
Yes. NFC plates simply open a URL -- the same as typing a web address into a browser. All payment processing happens through the giving platform's secure, PCI-compliant checkout. No financial data touches the NFC chip. The chip contains only a URL.

**What is the difference between locked and unlocked NFC plates?**
Locked plates have their NFC memory set to read-only after encoding the URL. Anyone can still tap and open the link, but the data cannot be overwritten. Unlocked plates allow anyone with physical access and an NFC phone to rewrite the destination URL.

**Should I lock or leave my NFC tap plates unlocked?**
Tap.Giving recommends locking plates (the default option). Locked plates prevent tampering, keep donor journeys consistent, and eliminate maintenance. If the destination needs to change, simply update the redirect on your domain instead of reprogramming the plate.

**What is the lock-then-redirect method?**
Program a branded URL you control onto the NFC plate, then lock the chip. Use your website or domain registrar to redirect that URL to any current campaign page. Change the destination anytime without touching the physical plate.

**How do I password protect unlocked NFC plates?**
Download the free NFC Tools app. Tap Other then Set Password, create a short passcode, and touch the plate. To edit, choose Remove Password, enter the code, make changes, then set the password again. Note: passwords only block rewriting, not scanning.

### Pricing and Ordering Questions

**Are there any ongoing costs or subscriptions?**
No ongoing costs with Tap.Giving. You only pay the one-time hardware cost. Any processing fees come from your chosen giving platform (Tithely, Subsplash, etc.).

**What's the minimum order quantity?**
100 plates. Most churches find they need 1-3 plates per pew or seating row for optimal coverage.

**Can I customize the plates with our church logo?**
Yes. Custom branding is included with every package. A design proof is sent for approval before production.

**What if I need to order more plates later?**
Additional plates can be ordered anytime. Branding designs are kept on file for easy reordering.

**Do you offer any guarantees?**
Yes. 30-day satisfaction guarantee. If not completely satisfied, return unused plates for a full refund.

### Technical Questions

**How long do the plates last?**
NFC chips have no battery and no moving parts. They last essentially forever. NTAG chips are rated for 100,000+ read cycles. The printed plates are durable, waterproof, and designed for long-term mounting.

**Can the URL be changed later?**
If plates are shipped unlocked, yes -- reprogram with any NFC writing app. If locked (recommended), the URL is permanent. Use the lock-then-redirect method to change the destination without touching the plate.

**Do the plates need to be charged or plugged in?**
No. The plates are completely passive NFC devices that require no power, charging, or maintenance. Place them on pews and they're ready to go.

**Will phone cases block NFC?**
Standard cases (plastic, leather, silicone, TPU) do not block NFC signals. Only very thick metal cases or RFID-blocking wallets interfere, representing less than 1% of cases in use.

**Do the plates need Wi-Fi?**
The plate itself does not need any network connection. The NFC exchange is a direct, local radio interaction. The phone needs data connectivity (Wi-Fi or cellular) to load the giving page after the tap.

### Setup and Installation Questions

**How long does setup take?**
Setup takes 5-10 minutes. Everything is pre-configured to work with the church's existing giving platform before shipping. Mounting is just peel-and-stick.

**How long does shipping take?**
3-5 weeks for standard orders, including production time for custom-branded plates plus shipping. Rush orders available upon request.

**Where should we place the NFC plates?**
Most popular: chair/pew backs. Also recommended: entry/exit areas, connection tables, lobby displays, bulletins/programs, children's ministry check-in. Start with every other row or high-traffic areas, then expand.

**What payment methods do donors use?**
Donors give using whatever methods their church's giving platform supports: Apple Pay, Google Pay, PayPal, or any major credit/debit card. The checkout process typically takes 15-30 seconds. Recurring giving options are also available.

### Church Giving Technology Questions

**What is the most cost-effective giving technology for churches?**
NFC tap-to-give plates are the most cost-effective option for in-service giving. Tap.Giving plates cost $3.50-$4.50 each as a one-time purchase with no monthly fees. A church with 200 seats can be fully equipped for $800 total. Compare that to giving apps ($119-$1,475/month) or kiosks ($1,500-$3,000 per unit plus monthly software fees).

**What percentage of church giving is digital in 2026?**
Approximately 49% of church giving is done via credit card or other digital means, with 80% of all donations made by debit or credit card. Cash giving has declined to about 40% of all church donations.

**Do churches need multiple giving technologies?**
Yes. Research shows that churches offering multiple ways to give are nearly 2x as likely to report increased giving. The key is covering both in-service giving (NFC plates, cash) and remote giving (online giving page, recurring gifts).

**How does NFC tap-to-give compare to QR codes?**
NFC generates 42x more engagement than QR codes in head-to-head tests. NFC requires one step (tap phone) vs. QR which requires opening a camera, scanning, and tapping a link. NFC works in dim sanctuaries where QR codes struggle and feels more natural since people already tap their phones to pay at stores.

**What church giving technology works best for first-time visitors?**
NFC tap-to-give is the most visitor-friendly technology because it requires zero setup -- no app download, no account creation, no typing. 53% of NFC givers are first-time givers. Visitors won't download an app for a church they've visited once.

**Can NFC plates work with our existing giving platform?**
Yes. NFC plates are platform-agnostic -- they open a URL in the phone's browser. If your giving platform has a web-based giving page (virtually all do), NFC plates work with it. Tap.Giving plates work with Tithely, Pushpay, Givelify, Donorbox, Planning Center, Anedot, Realm, Breeze, Nucleus, and any other platform with an online giving page.

**Do I need to replace my current giving platform to use NFC?**
No. NFC plates work with whatever platform your church already uses. The plates simply open your existing giving URL when tapped.

**How much does church giving technology cost per year?**
QR codes are free. NFC plates are a one-time $450-$1,400 purchase. Text-to-give runs $50-$200/month ($600-$2,400/year). Online platforms range from free (with transaction fees) to $1,475/month for Pushpay. Giving kiosks cost $1,500-$5,000 per unit. All-in-one ChMS platforms range from $50-$500+/month.

**Can our church use multiple giving technologies at the same time?**
Absolutely -- and most churches should. The best strategy combines 2-3 methods: NFC plates on pews for in-service giving, an online giving page for remote/recurring gifts, and optionally text-to-give or QR codes as backup.

**What is the best church donation technology for small churches on a budget?**
NFC tap-to-give plates. Tap.Giving plates cost $3.50-$4.50 each with no monthly fees, so a 100-seat church can be fully equipped for $450 one time. Pair with a free-tier online platform like Tithely or Donorbox for digital giving outside of services.

---
